body: A plane curve can be described by a pair of functions \(x = f(t)\) and \(y = g(t)\) where \(t\) is a parameter, often representing time. The velocity components are \(dx/dt = f'(t)\) and \(dy/dt = g'(t)\). When the curve is traced exactly once as \(t\) runs from \(\alpha\) to \(\beta\), the arc‑length formula is

title: Lamar University – Arc Length with Parametric Equations
relevance: Provides the formal statement of the parametric arc‑length formula and the assumptions required for its use.

body: Consider an infinitesimal segment of the curve. By the Pythagorean theorem, its length \(ds\) satisfies \(ds^{2}=dx^{2}+dy^{2}\). Dividing by \(dt^{2}\) gives \((ds/dt)^{2}= (dx/dt)^{2}+(dy/dt)^{2}\). Taking the positive square root and integrating from \(\alpha\) to \(\beta\) yields the arc‑length integral.

text: Example: Length of One Loop of a Cycloid

body: A cycloid generated by a circle of radius \(a\) has the parametric equations \(x = a(\theta-\sin\theta)\) and \(y = a(1-\cos\theta)\).

items: Compute the derivatives: \(dx/d\theta = a(1-\cos\theta)\) and \(dy/d\theta = a\sin\theta\).

title: ProofWiki – Length of Arc of Cycloid
relevance: Derives the exact length \(8a\) of a single cycloid arch using the parametric formula.

items: Cartesian: Requires solving \(y = f(x)\) for \(dy/dx\) and integrating \(\sqrt{1+(dy/dx)^{2}}\) with respect to \(x\). Often leads to messy algebra or implicit integrals.
Parametric: Directly uses \(dx/dt\) and \(dy/dt\); the integral is usually simpler because the parameter follows the natural motion of the curve.
Example (cycloid): Cartesian form is implicit and hard, while the parametric approach gives a clean integral \(\int_{0}^{2\pi}2a\sin(\theta/2)\,d\theta\).

title: Cycloid Animation
caption: Use the slider \(t\) to trace one arch of the cycloid \(x = a(t-\sin t),\; y = a(1-\cos t)\).

questions.q: Given \(x = 3t^{2},\; y = 2t^{3}\) for \(0 \le t \le 1\), what is the arc length?
For the cycloid \(x = a(\theta-\sin\theta),\; y = a(1-\cos\theta)\), the integrand simplifies to:
Why does the parametric arc‑length formula often produce a simpler integral than the Cartesian version?
options: Because the parameter \(t\) follows the natural motion of the curve, avoiding implicit differentiation.
Because it eliminates the need for limits of integration.
Because it converts the problem to polar coordinates.
questions.explanation: The parameter aligns with the curve’s generation, making derivatives straightforward.

questions.q: If a curve is given by \(x = \cos t,\; y = \sin t\) for \(0 \le t \le 2\pi\), what is its total arc length?
questions.explanation: dx/dt = -sin t, dy/dt = cos t; integrand = \(\sqrt{\sin^{2}t+\cos^{2}t}=1\). Integral over \(0\) to \(2\pi\) gives \(2\pi\).

text: Engineering Insight: In civil engineering, the arc length of a road curve determines the amount of pavement and the placement of drainage. Accurate parametric calculations prevent costly over‑ or under‑estimation.

title: Ask the Tutor
persona: You are an engineering mathematician who explains how arc length relates to material usage, such as the length of a pipe needed to follow a designed path.
greeting: Hi! I can help you translate an arc‑length calculation into real‑world engineering quantities. Ask me anything about material estimation, safety factors, or converting parametric results into manufacturable specifications.
